one_line_takeaway: By combining program slicing, LLM-based semantic refinement of vulnerability-relevant statements, and AST-normalized statement-level clone detection to backtrack the true vulnerability-introducing commit, VERCATION reaches 93.1% F1 on a 122-CVE/1,211-version ground truth (vs. 51.4% F1 for raw NVD data and 80.1%-86.1% F1 for prior SOTA), and flagged 202 of 342 real-world NVD CVE version reports as incomplete or over-inclusive.
key_finding: On the 122-CVE/1,211-version ground-truth dataset, VERCATION achieved precision 91.8%, recall 94.5%, F1 93.1%, beating the best baseline V-SZZ (F1 80.1%, a 16.2% relative F1 improvement) and Vision (F1 86.1%); raw NVD/CPE labels scored only precision 66.8%/recall 41.8%/F1 51.4%. The Joern-only static-analysis ablation scored F1 48.3% and DeepSeek-LLM-only (whole function, no slicing) scored F1 76.8%/73.9% (two figures given in different table references), while the combined Joern+DeepSeek pipeline reached F1 93.1%, and Joern alone extracted 3.64x more (mostly irrelevant) statements per CVE than the combined approach. DeepSeek-V3 with Few-shot+CoT was the best-performing LLM configuration (F1 93.1%, vs. GPT-4 92.5% and CodeLlama 87.6%), and also had the highest run-to-run agreement rate (58.6%) and Jaccard similarity (0.629) across 10 stochastic runs. The statement weighting ablation found the proposed 2:1 (Wsensitive:Wbase) ratio optimal at F1 93.1% vs. 89.6% for equal (1:1) weighting. In the code-clone-detection component ablation, VERCATION's AST-based statement-level method reached precision 0.90/recall 0.95, beating edit distance (0.85/0.84), MD5 hashing (0.82/0.82), CodeBERT embeddings (0.84/0.88), and the function-level FCDetector (0.89/0.93); 32.2% of the 276 pre/post-commit comparisons performed during backtracking were classified as refactoring commits that plain edit-distance methods would have mishandled. Applying VERCATION to an independently collected set of 342 real-world NVD CVEs, it flagged 202 (59.1%) as having incorrect vulnerable-version information -- 134 with incomplete CPE ranges (108 reporting only a single vulnerable version) and 68 with over-inclusive CPE ranges (56 flagging all pre-report versions as vulnerable without justification) -- and found that CVEs with Medium (49.7%) and High (49.1%) CVSS severity accounted for most of the incomplete-version cases, including 2 Critical-severity cases in FFmpeg and PHP; these findings were reported to NVD. Average per-CVE analysis time was 28.61 seconds (vs. an estimated ~30 days for exhaustive PoC-based dynamic testing across the same 1,211-version dataset, a claimed ~742x speedup), and the average DeepSeek-V3 API cost per CVE (10 self-consistency runs) was $0.0034.
core_contribution: VERCATION is an automated pipeline for precisely identifying which released versions of C/C++ open-source software (OSS) are affected by a given CVE, given only the vulnerability-fixing commit (vfc). It combines (1) program slicing on the patched function's program dependency graph to extract candidate 'dangerous flow' statements (backward slicing to trace patch-related variables, forward slicing to find trigger behavior, with custom rules per statement type: assignment, conditional, function call, return), (2) an LLM (GPT-4, CodeLlama-13B, or DeepSeek-V3) prompted with few-shot examples and Chain-of-Thought to refine the dangerous flow into the actual vulnerability-relevant statements (avoiding recall of memorized CVE facts -- the LLM is instructed to reason about code semantics, not recite training data), (3) a weight-allocation scheme that up-weights statements calling functions from a hand-curated 'sensitive function' table (e.g., strcpy/memcpy for buffer overflow, malloc/free for use-after-free) at Wsensitive=2*Wbase, and (4) a statement-level code clone detection method that backtracks git history via git blame, using edit distance for a fast syntactic pre-filter (threshold theta1=0.9) and, when that fails, an expanded/normalized Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) similarity comparison (threshold theta2=0.8) -- expansion inlines called functions (e.g., a FFMIN macro) so refactored/outlined code is still recognized as equivalent, and normalization canonicalizes switch-case into if-else, all loops into while, and commutative operand order. A combined weighted similarity score (threshold theta3=0.7) determines whether a historical commit is the vulnerability-introducing commit (vic); the vulnerable version set is then computed as the set-difference between version tags reachable from the vic and version tags reachable from the vfc.
threat_model: Not an attacker/defender threat model in the adversarial-ML sense; this is a supply-chain/vulnerability-management problem. The 'threat' addressed is that public vulnerability databases (specifically NVD/CPE) frequently mislabel which OSS versions are affected by a CVE -- either by over-including all versions prior to a patch (false positives) or under-including versions where a fix was delayed or backported (false negatives) -- which causes downstream software consumers to either over-patch unnecessarily or, more dangerously, fail to patch software that is actually vulnerable, leaving real security exposure. The paper does not model an active adversary attacking VERCATION itself; the analysis targets known, already-disclosed CVEs and their public patches.
limitations: Paper-stated Threats to Validity: (1) Internal validity -- VERCATION depends on the Joern parser to build code-property graphs, and Joern struggles with complex pointer arithmetic, overloaded constructors, and indirect calls, so some data flows/constructor edges are missed; the AST-expansion (method-inlining) step cannot always resolve targets reached via function pointers, callbacks, or C++ virtual dispatch. (2) Multi-function patches are analyzed per-function independently and then merged, which can miss vulnerability patterns that depend on data/control flow spanning multiple functions. (3) LLM variability/reproducibility -- performance may drift as underlying LLMs evolve, and LLM training-data biases could affect understanding of certain vulnerability types; consistency across 10 runs was only moderate (agreement rates 45.4%-58.6%). (4) External validity -- language generalizability beyond C/C++ is untested (the method is claimed to be language-agnostic in principle since Joern supports other languages, but this is not empirically validated in the paper). (5) Complex diamond-shaped merge/branching scenarios where the same vulnerable code is independently modified on parallel feature branches may require more sophisticated graph-traversal logic than the current multi-parent backtracking supports. The paper also notes VERCATION still misidentifies vic in some cases where AST-similarity is insensitive to small-but-security-critical edits, or where the sensitive-function weighting misassigns high weight to patch-adjacent-but-irrelevant statements.

datasets_used: Curated ground-truth dataset: 122 CVEs, 1,211 versions, 12 OSS projects (Binutils 157v/18CVE, cJSON 48v/3CVE, FFmpeg 405v/31CVE, Jasper 99v/12CVE, Libarchive 51v/10CVE, Libcaca 7v/3CVE, Liblouis 69v/6CVE, Libming 14v/9CVE, Libtiff 78v/7CVE, Libxml2 233v/12CVE, OpenJPEG 25v/8CVE, Pcre2 25v/3CVE), CVEs dated 2016-2025, 13 CWE types, labeled via PoC execution + manual verification (10 days build + 30 days labeling)
Real-world application dataset: 342 additional CVEs crawled from NVD with corresponding CPE and patches
quantitative_results: Vulnerability-extraction ablation (Table IV): Joern-only F1 0.483 (precision 0.435/recall 0.544, avg 22.64 stmts/CVE), DeepSeek-only F1 0.768 (0.742/0.796, avg 11.33 stmts/CVE), Joern+DeepSeek F1 0.931 (0.918/0.945, avg 5.66 stmts/CVE); Joern alone extracts 3.64x more statements per CVE than the combined method

LLM+prompting strategy comparison (Table V): GPT-4 zero-shot F1 0.761, few-shot 0.899, few-shot+CoT 0.925; CodeLlama zero-shot 0.725, few-shot 0.873, few-shot+CoT 0.876; DeepSeek-V3 zero-shot 0.797, few-shot 0.901, few-shot+CoT 0.931 (best)
LLM consistency across 10 runs (Table VI): GPT-4 agreement rate 0.454/Jaccard 0.439; CodeLlama 0.517/0.566; DeepSeek-V3 0.586/0.629 (highest)
Knowledge-cutoff generalization (Table VII): CodeLlama pre/post-cutoff F1 0.881 to 0.870 (down 1.25%); GPT-4 0.923 to 0.928 (up 0.54%); DeepSeek-V3 0.927 to 0.943 (up 1.73%)
Statement weighting ratio sensitivity: 1:1 ratio F1 0.896; proposed 2:1 ratio F1 0.931 (3.91% improvement); F1 declines for ratios beyond 2:1
Code clone detection accuracy (Table VIII): edit distance 0.85/0.84 (P/R), hash values 0.82/0.82, CodeBERT 0.84/0.88, FCDetector (function-level) 0.89/0.93, VERCATION (statement-level, AST) 0.90/0.95

Refactoring-commit analysis: 276 total pre/post-commit comparisons during backtrack, avg 3.73 comparisons per vfc to locate vic; 89 commits (32.2%) classified as refactoring commits; only 11% of vic-vfc pairs had zero intermediate commits
Non-linear/merge-commit subset (5.7% of traced histories): VERCATION precision 91.2%, recall 92.5%

Real-world NVD audit: of 342 CVEs, 202 (59.1%) had incorrect vulnerable-version info; 134 incomplete (108 reporting only a single vulnerable version), 68 over-inclusive (56 flagging all pre-report versions); severity breakdown of the 134 incomplete cases: 49.7% Medium (CVSS 4.0-6.9), 49.1% High (7.0-8.9), 2 Critical (9.0-10.0, in FFmpeg and PHP); 4 CVEs found with no vulnerable-version info at all

Performance/cost: avg 28.61 sec/CVE for VERCATION vs. 10.25 sec (V-SZZ), ~22 sec (V0Finder), 1,094.12 sec (Vision), <20 sec for 99% of projects (V1SCAN), 0.71 sec (VERJava, but much lower recall); estimated ~30 days for exhaustive dynamic/PoC testing of the 122-CVE dataset vs. 28.61 sec/CVE static approach (~742x claimed speedup); avg LLM API cost per CVE (DeepSeek-V3, 10 runs) = $0.0034; only 24% of NVD CVEs (1999-2025) have public exploit links, and only 55% of available PoCs in their dataset executed successfully across multiple versions without modification
